news_summary: Scientists are making groundbreaking discoveries by creating new forms of matter, uncovering genetic secrets of historical geniuses, revealing hidden structures of advanced materials, and advancing innovative dark matter detection techniques.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques font des découvertes révolutionnaires en créant de nouvelles formes de matière, en perçant les secrets génétiques de génies de l'histoire, en dévoilant les structures cachées de matériaux de pointe et en développant des techniques innovantes de détection de la matière noire.
news_summary_es: Los científicos están realizando descubrimientos revolucionarios al crear nuevas formas de materia, desvelar los secretos genéticos de genios de la historia, revelar estructuras ocultas de materiales avanzados y desarrollar técnicas innovadoras para la detección de la materia oscura.

articles.title: Scientists just created exotic new forms of matter that shouldn’t exist
articles.summary: A new quantum physics study reveals that simply changing a magnetic field over time can unlock entirely new forms of matter that don’t exist under normal conditions. By carefully “driving” materials with timed magnetic shifts, researchers created exotic quantum states that could be far more stable and resistant to errors—one of the biggest challenges in quantum computing. This breakthrough suggests that the future of quantum technology may depend not just on what materials are made of, but how they’re manipulated in time.
articles.content:
Quantum technology is widely expected to transform how large and complex data sets are processed. Although it is currently used mostly in laboratories and research environments, the field is steadily moving toward real-world applications across a range of industries.

In a recent study exploring the fundamentals of quantum physics, researchers examined how matter behaves at extremely small scales, including atoms, electrons, and photons. The work, led by Cal Poly Physics Department Lecturer Ian Powell, focused on how varying a magnetic field over time can cause matter to exhibit unusual and previously unseen properties.

Powell and student researcher Louis Buchalter, who earned a Cal Poly bachelor's degree in physics in 2025, published their findings in Physical Review B in a paper titled "Flux-Switching Floquet Engineering." Their research shows that when magnetic fields are changed in a controlled, time-dependent way, they can generate quantum states that do not exist in materials that remain unchanged over time (remaining in the same state as time elapses).

"On a big-picture level, I would describe this as an advance in our understanding of how time-dependent control can create and organize new forms of quantum matter," Powell said. "The central idea is that useful quantum properties can depend not just on what a material is, but on how it is driven in time. In our case, we show that periodically changing a magnetic field can produce driven quantum phases with no static counterpart."

Toward More Stable Quantum Technologies

By carefully timing how magnetic fields are applied, scientists can design quantum systems with properties that are more stable and less vulnerable to "noise" or imperfections. These disruptions are a major challenge in quantum technology, often leading to errors in calculations or system performance.

Powell noted that while the technical details can be difficult to explain outside the field, the broader concept is clear. The findings suggest new ways to create and study these unusual quantum states in controlled settings such as ultracold-atom experiments.

"The most direct industry relevance of our study is to quantum computing and quantum simulation, rather than to a specific end-use sector at this stage," Powell said. "Any eventual impact on areas like pharmaceuticals, finance, manufacturing or aerospace would likely be indirect, by contributing to the longer-term development of better quantum technologies. To move toward industry use, the next steps would be experimental validation and further work connecting these ideas to realistic quantum-device platforms."

New Mathematical Patterns in Quantum Systems

Beyond creating new quantum states, the research also identified a mathematical organizing principle that mirrors patterns typically found in higher-dimensional quantum systems. This suggests that relatively simple systems driven by changing conditions could provide new ways to explore more complex quantum physics.

The team also mapped out how these exotic states form, revealing a precise structure in the system's topological phase diagram. This diagram serves as a visual guide to different stable quantum phases, each defined by fixed topological properties.

Why Quantum Control Matters for Computing

Quantum mechanics allows computing systems to process information in ways that far exceed the capabilities of classical computers. These systems can perform large-scale simulations, analyze vast data sets, and solve complex problems more efficiently.

Magnetic fields play a central role in this process. They are commonly used to control and measure quantum bits (or qubits), the fundamental units of quantum information. Qubits are comparable to the units of 0s and 1s in classicalcomputing (applied in commonplace computing currently) used to represent physical electrical states.

Student Research Experience and Future Work

For Buchalter, participating in the study provided valuable insight into the research process and scientific communication.

"A lot about the process of conducting research and how new research findings are effectively communicated with the broader scientific community."

"I learned that research is rarely a straightforward process, often requiring persistence and creative problem solving during the course of a research project," Buchalter said. "I believe our results help demonstrate the power of Floquet engineering for realizing quantum systems with highly-tunable properties, paving the way for further research into periodically driven quantum matter and the development of its applications."

Buchalter plans to begin a Master of Science program in materials science and engineering at the University of Washington in the fall, where he will focus on experimental studies of quantum matter. He is also considering a future career at a national laboratory working on quantum device development.

"I initially took on the project due to my interest in condensed matter physics, however, I became fascinated with the field of quantum materials through my experience," Buchalter said. "I am very interested in continuing to study quantum matter and helping develop its applications in electronic and photonic devices."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in quantum physics where researchers created new exotic forms of matter by manipulating magnetic fields over time. This discovery has broad implications for the future stability and error-resistance of quantum computing technologies, which could impact many industries and advance quantum technology development. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights meaningful progress in a cutting-edge field with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create new forms of matter by changing magnetic fields over time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study quantum physics to understand how tiny particles like atoms and electrons behave. Quantum technology uses these particles to build powerful computers and tools.

**What happened** – Researchers at Cal Poly, led by Ian Powell and student Louis Buchalter, found that by changing magnetic fields in a timed way, they could create new states of matter that don’t appear normally. They published their findings in a scientific journal called Physical Review B.

**Impact** – These new forms of matter could help make quantum computers more stable and less likely to make mistakes. This is important because current quantum computers often have errors due to outside interference. The discovery also opens new ways to explore complex quantum physics using simpler materials.

**What's next step** – Scientists will try to test these findings in experiments and connect them to real quantum devices. This could eventually lead to better quantum computers and technologies used in different industries like medicine or manufacturing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Changing magnetic fields over time can create new, stable quantum states that may improve future quantum computing technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent de nouvelles formes de matière en modifiant les champs magnétiques dans le temps
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient la physique quantique pour comprendre le comportement des toutes petites particules comme les atomes et les électrons. La technologie quantique utilise ces particules pour construire des ordinateurs et des outils très puissants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Cal Poly, dirigés par Ian Powell et l’étudiant Louis Buchalter, ont découvert qu’en changeant les champs magnétiques de façon rythmée, ils pouvaient créer de nouveaux états de la matière qui n’existent pas normalement. Ils ont publié leurs résultats dans la revue scientifique Physical Review B.

**Impact** – Ces nouvelles formes de matière pourraient rendre les ordinateurs quantiques plus stables et moins sujets aux erreurs. C’est important car les ordinateurs quantiques actuels font souvent des erreurs à cause des interférences extérieures. Cette découverte ouvre aussi de nouvelles façons d’explorer la physique quantique complexe avec des matériaux plus simples.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont essayer de vérifier ces découvertes par des expériences et de les appliquer à des dispositifs quantiques réels. Cela pourrait conduire à de meilleurs ordinateurs quantiques et technologies pour différents secteurs comme la médecine ou l’industrie.

**Une phrase clé** – Modifier les champs magnétiques dans le temps permet de créer de nouveaux états quantiques stables qui pourraient améliorer la technologie quantique future.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean nuevas formas de materia al cambiar campos magnéticos en el tiempo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian la física cuántica para entender cómo se comportan partículas muy pequeñas como átomos y electrones. La tecnología cuántica usa estas partículas para construir computadoras y herramientas muy potentes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Cal Poly, dirigidos por Ian Powell y el estudiante Louis Buchalter, descubrieron que al cambiar campos magnéticos de manera controlada en el tiempo, podían crear nuevos estados de la materia que no existen normalmente. Publicaron sus hallazgos en la revista científica Physical Review B.

**Impacto** – Estas nuevas formas de materia podrían ayudar a que las computadoras cuánticas sean más estables y cometan menos errores. Esto es importante porque las computadoras cuánticas actuales suelen tener errores por interferencias externas. El descubrimiento también abre nuevas formas de explorar la física cuántica compleja con materiales más simples.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos intentarán probar estos resultados en experimentos y conectarlos con dispositivos cuánticos reales. Esto podría llevar a mejores computadoras y tecnologías cuánticas para diferentes industrias como medicina o manufactura.

**Una frase clave** – Cambiar campos magnéticos en el tiempo puede crear nuevos estados cuánticos estables que podrían mejorar la tecnología cuántica futura.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of glowing, interwoven magnetic field lines gently pulsing over a stylized lattice of abstract quantum particles, with swirling waves of light symbolizing time-dependent control, set against a soft, natural-toned backdrop evoking the subtle complexity and harmony of newly discovered quantum states.

articles.title: The da Vinci bloodline is unlocking the genius’s genetic secrets
articles.summary: After centuries of mystery, scientists are edging closer to uncovering Leonardo da Vinci’s biological secrets. A massive 30-year effort has mapped his family across 21 generations, identified living male descendants, and even confirmed shared DNA markers that stretch back to the Renaissance. Meanwhile, excavations at a family tomb in Vinci could provide ancient remains for comparison, raising the possibility of reconstructing Leonardo’s genetic profile.
articles.content:
For more than 500 years, Leonardo da Vinci has been admired as a brilliant artist, inventor, and thinker whose talents seemed far ahead of his time. Now, an ambitious international effort known as the Leonardo DNA Project is bringing scientists closer than ever to uncovering the biological roots of his genius.

A newly published book, "Genìa Da Vinci. Genealogy and Genetics for Leonardo's DNA," brings together three decades of research led by Alessandro Vezzosi and Agnese Sabato of the Leonardo Da Vinci Heritage Association in Vinci. Supported by the Municipality of Vinci, the work reconstructs an extensive family tree stretching back to 1331. It spans 21 generations and includes more than 400 individuals, creating the foundation for an unprecedented attempt to rebuild Leonardo's genetic profile.

By carefully studying archival records and historical documents, the researchers were able to map out previously unknown branches of Leonardo's family. In the process, they identified 15 living male descendants linked directly through the paternal line to Leonardo's father and his half-brother, Domenico Benedetto.

DNA Testing Links Living Descendants

This discovery opened the door for genetic analysis. David Caramelli, who coordinates the anthropological and molecular aspects of the Leonardo DNA Project and leads the Department of Biology at the University of Florence, worked with forensic anthropologist Elena Pilli to analyze DNA from six of these descendants.

The results showed that segments of the Y chromosome matched across the participants. Because this chromosome is passed from father to son with little change, the findings confirm a continuous male lineage within the Da Vinci family dating back at least 15 generations.

Ancient Tomb Could Hold Crucial Evidence

Researchers have also identified a Da Vinci family tomb at the Church of Santa Croce in Vinci. Archaeological excavations are currently underway in collaboration with the University of Florence. The site is believed to contain the remains of Leonardo's grandfather Antonio, his uncle Francesco, and several half-brothers, Antonio, Pandolfo, and Giovanni.

Anthropologists Alessandro Riga and Luca Bachechi have recovered bone fragments from the site, some of which have been radiocarbon dated. One specimen, consistent in age with Leonardo's relatives, has already undergone paleogenomic testing. Early analysis suggests the individual was male.

"Further detailed analyses are necessary to determine whether the DNA extracted is sufficiently preserved," says Caramelli, who is also President of the University Museum System. "Based on the results, we can proceed with analysis of Y chromosome fragments for comparison with current descendants."

If the Y chromosome from these remains matches that of living descendants, it would strengthen historical records and family lineage reconstructions. It could also make it possible to analyze biological traces connected to Leonardo himself, including material left on manuscripts or artworks, potentially enabling scientists to reconstruct his DNA.

A Global Scientific Effort

The Leonardo da Vinci DNA Project began in 2016 and is coordinated from The Rockefeller University in New York. It brings together institutions including the J. Craig Venter Institute in California and the University of Florence, with support from foundations such as the Achelis and Bodman Foundation (New York) and the Richard Lounsbery Foundation (Washington, D.C.).

The project focuses on tracking the Y chromosome, which passes largely unchanged through generations of males.

"Our goal in reconstructing the Da Vinci family's lineage up to the present day, while also preserving and valuing the places connected to Leonardo, is to enable scientific research on his DNA," says Vezzosi. "Through the recovery of Leonardo's DNA, we hope to understand the biological roots of his extraordinary visual acuity, creativity, and possibly even aspects of his health and causes of death."

"Even a tiny fingerprint on a page could contain cells to sequence," says Jesse H. Ausubel of The Rockefeller University and director of the project. "21st-century biology is moving the boundary between the unknowable and the unknown. Soon we may gain information about Leonardo and other historical figures once believed lost forever."

Beyond DNA: New Insights Into Leonardo's Life

The book goes far beyond genetics, offering a detailed exploration of Leonardo's world. Across 21 chapters, it examines historical, geographical, and genealogical evidence to better understand the environment in which he lived.

Researchers identified seven Da Vinci family homes in the village and castle of Vinci, along with two properties once owned by Leonardo himself. These properties were inherited from his uncle Francesco and were the subject of a long dispute with his half-brothers.

The study also revisits key figures in Leonardo's life. His grandfather Antonio is revealed as a traveling merchant who operated between Catalan Spain and Morocco, rather than simply a farmer. Meanwhile, new archival analysis provides a clearer view of Leonardo's mother, Caterina. Evidence suggests she may have been a slave working for a wealthy banker, Vanni di Niccolò di ser Vanni. Historical documents, including wills and donation records dating back to 1449, shed light on the relationship between this banker and Leonardo's father, ser Piero.

A Mysterious Drawing Emerges

A particularly intriguing finding involves a charcoal drawing discovered on the fireplace mantle of an old building in Vinci, once known as the Bracci house. The artwork depicts a fantastical creature combining multiple striking features, including a spiral horn, elongated snout, hooked teeth, wings, and a serpentine tail.

Vezzosi and Sabato have named the image the "Unicorn Dragon." Some elements appear to anticipate Leonardo's later studies of flight. Comparisons with a known drawing from the 1470s suggest it could be an early work by Leonardo, although further scientific analysis and restoration are planned.

The hypothesis has gained support from Roberta Barsanti, Director of the Leonardian Museum and Library, and Vinci's Mayor, Daniele Vanni. The Municipality is preparing a detailed examination of the large drawing (about 80x70 cm) under the supervision of cultural heritage authorities.

Leonardo and Early Ideas About Genetics

The book also explores Leonardo's own thinking about heredity. His writings suggest he may have anticipated ideas now associated with epigenetics, considering how diet, blood, and parental behavior might influence offspring.

"Leonardo questioned the origins of human life not only biologically: in his studies on generation, conception becomes a complex act where nature, emotion, and fate intertwine -- anticipating themes now central to the genetics-epigenetics debate," explains Agnese Sabato.

Toward Reconstructing Leonardo's DNA

The final sections of the research consider whether modern descendants share physical similarities with Leonardo's famous self-portrait. While this remains speculative, the main goal is scientific.

If enough genetic material can be recovered and sequenced, researchers hope to uncover details about Leonardo's biological traits, including aspects of his appearance, health, and even potential predispositions.

"This is not just about the author of the world's most famous painting," concludes Ausubel. "It's a challenge to redefine the limits of historical knowledge and cultural heritage."

Rebuilding Leonardo's genetic profile would mark a major milestone in both science and cultural history. For Vinci, the small Tuscan town where Leonardo was born, the possibility of hearing his "genetic voice" centuries later has become a powerful source of pride.

The research will also contribute to an upcoming documentary and an international film project. And despite everything uncovered so far, one conclusion stands out: Leonardo da Vinci's story is still unfolding.

Key Points
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific and cultural breakthrough involving the reconstruction of Leonardo da Vinci's genetic profile through a large-scale, multi-decade international research project. This effort has broad significance for science, history, and cultural heritage, with potential to unlock new knowledge about a universally admired historical figure. The story focuses on a single, well-detailed topic with clear real-world impact in genetics, anthropology, and historical understanding.
articles.personality_title: Scientists unlock Leonardo da Vinci’s family DNA to reveal his genetic story
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Leonardo da Vinci is famous for his art and inventions from over 500 years ago. Until now, very little was known about his family’s genetics or biological background.

**What happened** – For 30 years, scientists have studied Leonardo’s family tree, tracing 21 generations and identifying 15 living male relatives. By testing their DNA, they found shared markers on the Y chromosome passed from father to son. Archaeologists are also digging up a Da Vinci family tomb in Vinci, Italy, hoping to find ancient bones to compare DNA. This work is part of the Leonardo DNA Project, involving researchers from different countries.

**Impact** – This is the first time anyone has connected Leonardo’s family history with real genetic evidence. If the ancient DNA matches living relatives, scientists might reconstruct Leonardo’s own DNA. This could help understand his unique talents and health better. It also shows how history and science can work together to learn new facts about famous people.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue testing DNA from the tomb and compare it with living relatives. They plan to study any biological material left on Leonardo’s artworks or documents. A new documentary and film project will also share these discoveries with the public.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After decades of research, scientists are close to unlocking Leonardo da Vinci’s genetic secrets by linking his family’s DNA across centuries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques décryptent l’ADN familial de Léonard de Vinci pour révéler son histoire génétique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Léonard de Vinci est célèbre pour ses œuvres d’art et ses inventions datant de plus de 500 ans. Jusqu’à présent, on connaissait peu de choses sur la génétique ou les origines biologiques de sa famille.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pendant 30 ans, des scientifiques ont étudié l’arbre généalogique de Léonard, retraçant 21 générations et identifiant 15 parents masculins vivants. En analysant leur ADN, ils ont découvert des marqueurs partagés sur le chromosome Y transmis de père en fils. Des archéologues fouillent également une tombe familiale à Vinci, en Italie, pour trouver des ossements anciens à comparer. Ce travail fait partie du Projet ADN Léonard, réunissant des chercheurs de plusieurs pays.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’histoire familiale de Léonard est liée à des preuves génétiques réelles. Si l’ADN ancien correspond à celui des descendants, les scientifiques pourraient reconstruire l’ADN de Léonard lui-même. Cela pourrait aider à mieux comprendre ses talents uniques et sa santé. Cela montre aussi comment histoire et science s’unissent pour découvrir de nouveaux faits sur des personnages célèbres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à tester l’ADN de la tombe et le comparer avec celui des descendants vivants. Ils envisagent d’étudier tout matériel biologique laissé sur les œuvres ou documents de Léonard. Un nouveau documentaire et un projet de film partageront ces découvertes avec le public.

**En une phrase** – Après des décennies de recherche, les scientifiques sont proches de dévoiler les secrets génétiques de Léonard de Vinci en reliant l’ADN de sa famille à travers les siècles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos revelan el ADN familiar de Leonardo da Vinci para descubrir su historia genética
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Leonardo da Vinci es famoso por su arte e inventos de hace más de 500 años. Hasta ahora, se sabía muy poco sobre la genética o los orígenes biológicos de su familia.

**Qué pasó** – Durante 30 años, científicos estudiaron el árbol genealógico de Leonardo, rastreando 21 generaciones e identificando 15 parientes masculinos vivos. Al analizar su ADN, encontraron marcadores comunes en el cromosoma Y que se transmite de padre a hijo. Arqueólogos también excavan una tumba familiar en Vinci, Italia, para encontrar restos antiguos para comparar el ADN. Este trabajo forma parte del Proyecto ADN Leonardo, que reúne investigadores de varios países.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que la historia familiar de Leonardo se conecta con evidencia genética real. Si el ADN antiguo coincide con el de los descendientes, los científicos podrían reconstruir el ADN de Leonardo. Esto ayudaría a entender mejor sus talentos únicos y su salud. También muestra cómo la historia y la ciencia pueden unirse para descubrir nuevos hechos sobre personas famosas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán analizando el ADN de la tumba y comparándolo con el de los familiares vivos. También planean estudiar cualquier material biológico dejado en las obras o documentos de Leonardo. Un nuevo documental y proyecto de película compartirán estos descubrimientos con el público.

**En una frase** – Tras décadas de investigación, los científicos están cerca de revelar los secretos genéticos de Leonardo da Vinci al conectar el ADN de su familia a lo largo de los siglos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient family tree with intertwined DNA double helix strands forming its trunk and branches, set against the Tuscan hills of Vinci with a softly glowing medieval church in the background, and a mystical charcoal drawing of the "Unicorn Dragon" subtly integrated into the foliage.

articles.title: MIT scientists finally reveal the hidden structure of a mysterious high-tech material
articles.summary: For decades, relaxor ferroelectrics have powered everything from medical ultrasounds to sonar systems, yet their inner atomic structure remained a mystery—until now. Researchers have finally mapped their three-dimensional structure in unprecedented detail, uncovering hidden patterns in how electric charges are arranged at the nanoscale. The breakthrough not only challenges long-standing assumptions about how these materials behave but also allows scientists to refine the models used to design them.
articles.content:
Materials known as relaxor ferroelectrics have played an important role for decades in technologies such as ultrasound imaging, microphones, and sonar. Their unusual performance comes from the way atoms are arranged inside them. However, that internal structure has been extremely difficult to measure directly, leaving scientists to rely on incomplete models.

Now, researchers from MIT and collaborating institutions have, for the first time, mapped the three dimensional atomic structure of a relaxor ferroelectric. Their results, to be published in Science, offer a clearer foundation for improving the models used to design future computing systems, energy devices, and advanced sensors.

"Now that we have a better understanding of exactly what's going on, we can better predict and engineer the properties we want materials to achieve," says corresponding author James LeBeau, MIT's Kyocera Professor of Materials Science and Engineering. "The research community is still developing methods to engineer these materials, but in order to predict the properties those materials will have, you have to know if your model is right."

Revealing Hidden Charge Patterns in Complex Materials

In the study, the team used a cutting edge imaging method to examine how electric charges are distributed throughout the material. What they found challenged previous assumptions.

"We realized the chemical disorder we observed in our experiments was not fully considered previously," says co-first authors Michael Xu PhD '25 and Menglin Zhu, who are both postdocs at MIT. "Working with our collaborators, we were able to merge the experimental observations with simulations to refine the models and better predict what we see in experiments."

The research team also included Colin Gilgenbach and Bridget R. Denzer, MIT PhD students in materials science and engineering; Yubo Qi, an assistant professor at the University of Alabama at Birmingham; Jieun Kim, an assistant professor at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology; Jiahao Zhang, a former PhD student at the University of Pennsylvania; Lane W. Martin, a professor at Rice University; and Andrew M. Rappe, a professor at the University of Pennsylvania.

Probing Disordered Materials at the Atomic Scale

Computer models have long suggested that when an electric field is applied to relaxor ferroelectrics, interactions between positively and negatively charged atoms within tiny regions help create their strong energy storage and sensing abilities. Until now, those nanoscale regions could not be directly observed.

To investigate further, the researchers focused on a widely used material found in sensors, actuators, and defense systems, a lead magnesium niobate-lead titanate alloy. They applied an advanced technique called multi-slice electron ptychography (MEP). This method involves scanning a nanoscale beam of high energy electrons across the material and recording the diffraction patterns that result.

"We do this in a sequential way, and at each position, we acquire a diffraction pattern," Zhu explains. "That creates regions of overlap, and that overlap has enough information to use an algorithm to iteratively reconstruct three-dimensional information about the object and the electron wave function."

Using this approach, the team uncovered a layered hierarchy of chemical and polar structures, extending from individual atoms up to larger, mesoscopic features. They also discovered that regions with different polarization were significantly smaller than earlier simulations had predicted. By incorporating these observations into their models, the researchers were able to improve how well simulations match real world behavior.

"Previously, these models basically had random regions of polarization, but they didn't tell you how those regions correlate with each other," Xu says. "Now we can tell you that information, and we can see how individual chemical species modulate polarization depending on the charge state of atoms."

Toward Better Materials for Future Technologies

According to Zhu, the findings highlight the growing power of electron ptychography for exploring complex, disordered materials and could lead to new lines of research.

"This study is the first time in the electron microscope that we've been able to directly connect the three-dimensional polar structure of relaxor ferroelectrics with molecular dynamics calculations," Xu says. "It further proves you can get three-dimensional information out of the sample using this technique."

The team believes this method could eventually help scientists design materials with tailored electronic properties, improving technologies such as memory storage, sensing systems, and energy devices.

"Materials science is incorporating more complexity into the material design process -- whether that's for metal alloys or semiconductors -- as AI has improved and our computational tools have become more advanced," LeBeau says. "But if our models aren't accurate enough and we have no way to validate them, it's garbage in garbage out. This technique helps us understand why the material behaves the way it does and validate our models."

The research was supported in part by the U.S. Army Research Laboratory, the U.S. Office of Naval Research, the U.S. Department of War, and a National Science Graduate Fellowship. The work also made use of MIT.nano facilities.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where MIT researchers have, for the first time, mapped the 3D atomic structure of relaxor ferroelectrics, materials critical to many technologies. This discovery challenges previous assumptions and improves models for designing future advanced sensors, energy devices, and computing systems, representing a meaningful advancement with broad technological impact. The article provides detailed context about the methods, findings, and potential applications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: MIT researchers uncover 3D atomic structure of key high-tech material
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Relaxor ferroelectrics are special materials used in devices like ultrasounds, microphones, and sonar. They work because of how their atoms are arranged inside, but until now, scientists did not know their exact atomic structure.

**What happened** – Scientists at MIT and other institutions used a new imaging technique called multi-slice electron ptychography to map the three-dimensional atomic structure of a relaxor ferroelectric for the first time. They studied a material made of lead magnesium niobate-lead titanate, revealing hidden patterns in how electric charges are arranged at the nanoscale.

**Impact** – This discovery challenges previous ideas about how these materials behave. It shows chemical disorder and smaller regions of electric polarization than expected. With this new detailed information, scientists can improve computer models that predict how these materials will perform, helping design better sensors, energy devices, and computing systems.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will use this improved understanding to create materials with specific electronic properties. The imaging method could lead to new research and help develop advanced technologies in memory storage, sensing, and energy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – MIT scientists have, for the first time, mapped the hidden 3D atomic structure of relaxor ferroelectrics, improving models that could lead to better high-tech devices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les chercheurs du MIT révèlent la structure atomique 3D d’un matériau high-tech clé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ferroélectriques relaxeurs sont des matériaux utilisés dans des appareils comme les ultrasons, les microphones et le sonar. Leur fonctionnement dépend de l’arrangement de leurs atomes, mais leur structure atomique exacte était inconnue jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques du MIT et d’autres institutions ont utilisé une nouvelle technique d’imagerie appelée ptychographie électronique multi-coupe pour cartographier pour la première fois la structure atomique tridimensionnelle d’un ferroélectrique relaxeur. Ils ont étudié un matériau composé de niobate de plomb magnésium-titane de plomb, révélant des motifs cachés dans la répartition des charges électriques à l’échelle nanométrique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en question les idées précédentes sur le comportement de ces matériaux. Elle montre un désordre chimique et des régions de polarisation électrique plus petites que prévu. Avec ces nouvelles informations détaillées, les scientifiques peuvent améliorer les modèles informatiques qui prédisent les performances de ces matériaux, aidant à concevoir de meilleurs capteurs, dispositifs énergétiques et systèmes informatiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront cette meilleure compréhension pour créer des matériaux aux propriétés électroniques spécifiques. Cette méthode d’imagerie pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles recherches et aider au développement de technologies avancées dans le stockage de mémoire, la détection et l’énergie.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques du MIT ont cartographié pour la première fois la structure atomique 3D cachée des ferroélectriques relaxeurs, améliorant les modèles qui pourraient conduire à de meilleurs dispositifs high-tech.
articles.personality_title_es: Investigadores del MIT revelan la estructura atómica 3D de un material clave de alta tecnología
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los ferroeléctricos relajantes son materiales usados en dispositivos como ultrasonidos, micrófonos y sonar. Funcionan por cómo están organizados sus átomos, pero hasta ahora no se conocía su estructura atómica exacta.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del MIT y otras instituciones usaron una nueva técnica de imagen llamada ptychografía electrónica de múltiples capas para mapear por primera vez la estructura atómica tridimensional de un ferroeléctrico relajante. Estudiaron un material hecho de niobato de magnesio-plomo y titanio-plomo, descubriendo patrones ocultos en la distribución de cargas eléctricas a escala nanométrica.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo desafía ideas anteriores sobre cómo se comportan estos materiales. Muestra desorden químico y regiones de polarización eléctrica más pequeñas de lo que se pensaba. Con esta nueva información, los científicos pueden mejorar los modelos computacionales que predicen el desempeño de estos materiales, ayudando a diseñar mejores sensores, dispositivos energéticos y sistemas informáticos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este mejor entendimiento para crear materiales con propiedades electrónicas específicas. Esta técnica de imagen podría abrir nuevas líneas de investigación y ayudar a desarrollar tecnologías avanzadas en almacenamiento, detección y energía.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos del MIT han mapeado por primera vez la estructura atómica 3D oculta de los ferroeléctricos relajantes, mejorando modelos que podrían crear mejores dispositivos de alta tecnología.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an intricate three-dimensional lattice of glowing, interconnected atoms and electric charges subtly shifting within a translucent crystal structure, symbolizing the hidden internal architecture of a high-tech relaxor ferroelectric material, with soft natural hues of amber, teal, and ivory creating a harmonious, scientific yet inviting atmosphere.

articles.title: 300-year-old experiment could become world's best dark matter detector
articles.summary: An update to an experiment run by Henry Cavendish in 1773 could be a cheaper and faster way to spot a potential dark matter particle – and may be 10,000 times more sensitive
articles.content:
An update to an experiment run by Henry Cavendish in 1773 could be a cheaper and faster way to spot a potential dark matter particle – and may be 10,000 times more sensitive

Dark matter makes up more of our universe than ordinary matter ESA/Euclid/Euclid Consortium/NASA, image processing by M. Schirmer (MPIA, Heidelberg)

A centuries-old experiment could help accelerate the search for new and exotic particles, including those that make up dark matter.

In 1773, British scientist Henry Cavendish set up a simple experiment aimed at uncovering the nature of electromagnetism. It involved measuring the electric potential at the surface of two nested metal shells to discern how charged particles affect each other within them.

Now, Peter Graham at Stanford University in California and his colleagues say that reviving Cavendish’s experiment could help reveal an even more mysterious feature of our cosmos – the particles that make up dark matter.

Though dark matter comprises much more of our cosmos than the ordinary matter, we don’t know what it is made of. There are many theoretical proposals for what it could be, and experiments trying to find out range from using particle colliders to elaborate underground detectors.

Graham and his colleagues focused on one dark matter candidate called millicharged particles (mCPs), which, as the name suggests, have unusually tiny charges. The property of being charged makes them a good match for Cavendish’s centuries-old setup.

The team propose to replicate his nested shell design, apply voltage to the bigger, outer shell then measure the difference in voltage between it and the inner shell. Because mCPs are electrically charged, this measurement would reveal whether any are present in the experiment.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Henry Cavendish’s 18th century experiment was designed to explore electromagnetism Cavendish Laboratory

To aid this, the new experiment would include an accumulator device which would suck up all the charged particles from the room like a vacuum cleaner, bringing all potential mCPs into the setup, says team-member Harikrishnan Ramani at the University of Delaware.

The design is simpler and more affordable relative to other mCP searches, with its cost estimated to be under a million dollars or a thousandth of operating a particle accelerator for a year. The researchers’ calculations also show that it could be more sensitive than some particle accelerator experiments that will come online in the future.

Kevin Kelly at Texas A&M University says that some of the estimates in the researchers’ calculations are probably conservative, so the proposed experiment could ultimately be between 100 and 10,000 more sensitive than past methods, allowing them to detect mCPs with charges even tinier than previously assumed.

“This technique could be better than some of the things that me and others are [already] doing,” says Christopher Hill at the Ohio State University. Like Graham and Ramani, he estimates that the experiment could be built and completed much more quickly than, for example, a particle accelerator experiment, thus significantly shortening the time to a possibly huge discovery. “It would be a big step to understanding what much of the universe is made of, and how it works,” says Hill. He says that he is contemplating whether he could build a similar experiment with his own team.

The team are now ironing out the details of building and securing funding for the experiment. If it works, which Ramani says could happen as soon as two or three years, it will have one more benefit – mCPs could be extracted from the Cavendish device and studied afterwards. “You could store and gift people millicharged particles,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific innovation that revives a centuries-old experiment to potentially detect dark matter particles with unprecedented sensitivity. This breakthrough could accelerate understanding of the universe's composition, has broad scientific significance, and offers a simpler, more affordable method than existing technologies. The article provides detailed context on the experiment's design, potential impact, and timeline, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Reviving a 300-year-old experiment to find dark matter faster and cheaper
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 1773, scientist Henry Cavendish created a simple experiment using two metal shells to study electric charges. Today, scientists want to update this experiment to search for mysterious particles called dark matter, which make up most of the universe but remain unseen.

**What happened** – Researchers at Stanford University and other places plan to copy Cavendish’s metal shell setup. They will apply electricity to the outer shell and measure changes in the inner shell to detect tiny charged particles called millicharged particles (mCPs), which might be part of dark matter. They will also use a device to collect these particles from the air to improve detection.

**Impact** – This updated experiment could be 100 to 10,000 times more sensitive than current methods, including expensive particle accelerators. It is simpler and much cheaper, costing under a million dollars, which is tiny compared to other dark matter searches. If successful, it could speed up the discovery of what dark matter really is.

**What's next step** – The team is working on building the experiment and finding funding. They hope to complete it within two to three years. If it works, they could even collect and share the detected particles for further study.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are updating a 300-year-old experiment to create a cheaper and more sensitive way to detect dark matter particles, potentially speeding up our understanding of the universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Réinventer une expérience vieille de 300 ans pour détecter la matière noire plus rapidement et à moindre coût
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 1773, le scientifique Henry Cavendish a inventé une expérience simple avec deux coques métalliques pour étudier les charges électriques. Aujourd’hui, des chercheurs veulent moderniser cette expérience pour chercher des particules mystérieuses appelées matière noire, qui composent la majeure partie de l’univers mais restent invisibles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Stanford et d’autres institutions prévoient de reproduire le système de coques métalliques de Cavendish. Ils appliqueront de l’électricité à la coque extérieure et mesureront les changements dans la coque intérieure pour détecter de minuscules particules chargées nommées particules millichargées (mCP), qui pourraient faire partie de la matière noire. Ils utiliseront aussi un appareil pour aspirer ces particules présentes dans l’air afin d’améliorer la détection.

**Impact** – Cette expérience mise à jour pourrait être 100 à 10 000 fois plus sensible que les méthodes actuelles, y compris les accélérateurs de particules coûteux. Elle est plus simple et beaucoup moins chère, coûtant moins d’un million de dollars, ce qui est minime par rapport à d’autres recherches sur la matière noire. Si elle réussit, elle pourrait accélérer la découverte de la nature réelle de la matière noire.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe travaille à construire l’expérience et à trouver des financements. Ils espèrent la terminer dans deux à trois ans. Si elle fonctionne, ils pourraient même collecter et partager les particules détectées pour les étudier davantage.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques modernisent une expérience vieille de 300 ans pour créer une méthode moins chère et plus sensible pour détecter la matière noire, accélérant ainsi notre compréhension de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Reavivan un experimento de 300 años para detectar materia oscura más rápido y barato
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 1773, el científico Henry Cavendish creó un experimento simple con dos cáscaras metálicas para estudiar cargas eléctricas. Hoy, los científicos quieren actualizar este experimento para buscar partículas misteriosas llamadas materia oscura, que forman la mayor parte del universo pero no se pueden ver.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Stanford y otros lugares planean copiar el diseño de las cáscaras metálicas de Cavendish. Aplicarán electricidad a la cáscara exterior y medirán cambios en la interior para detectar partículas muy pequeñas con carga llamadas partículas milicargadas (mCP), que podrían ser parte de la materia oscura. También usarán un dispositivo para recoger estas partículas del aire y mejorar la detección.

**Impacto** – Este experimento actualizado podría ser de 100 a 10,000 veces más sensible que los métodos actuales, incluidos costosos aceleradores de partículas. Es más simple y mucho más barato, con un costo menor a un millón de dólares, muy bajo comparado con otras búsquedas de materia oscura. Si tiene éxito, podría acelerar el descubrimiento de qué es realmente la materia oscura.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo está trabajando en construir el experimento y conseguir fondos. Esperan completarlo en dos o tres años. Si funciona, podrían incluso recolectar y compartir las partículas detectadas para estudiarlas más.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos actualizan un experimento de 300 años para crear una forma más barata y sensible de detectar partículas de materia oscura, acelerando así nuestro conocimiento del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two nested metallic shells glowing softly with subtle electric currents flowing between them, surrounded by a gentle, swirling cloud of tiny, faintly charged particles being drawn inward like by an invisible vacuum, set against a simple, muted cosmic background symbolizing the search for dark matter.
